3A_Bibliographic.citation: Chaarani, B., Ortigara, J., Yuan, D., Loso, H., Potter, A., & Garavan, H. P. (2022). Association of video gaming with cognitive performance among children. JAMA Network Open, 5(10), e2235721.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: This paper was retracted and republished with corrections (Supplements 2 and 3 reference this).

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: A total of 2217 children (mean [SD] age, 119 [7.6] months; 9.91 [0.62] years; 1399 [63.1%] females) participated in this study. The final sample used in the SST analyses consisted of 1128 NVGs... and 679 VGs... The final sample used in the n-back analyses consisted of 1278 NVGs... and 800 gamers.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: SST sample: 1807 (1128 NVG + 679 VG); n-back sample: 2078 (1278 NVG + 800 VG). Total unique participants = 2217.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Video gaming group: Video Gamers (VG, >= 21 hours/week i.e. >= 3 hours/day) vs Non-Video Gamers (NVG, 0 hours/week)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: For each participant, a total weekly video-gaming score was derived as the sum of (video-gaming hours per weekday x 5) + (video-gaming hours per weekend day x 2). Using the video-gaming score, we defined a group of NVGs who never played video games (0 gaming hours per week) and a group of VGs who played a minimum of 3 hours per day (21 hours per week) or more.
3C_Exposure.notes: Extreme groups design: excluded moderate gamers. Only compared 0 h/wk vs >= 21 h/wk.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Cognitive task performance (SSRT, correct go RT, D' on n-back) and task-related BOLD fMRI activation
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Stop Signal Task (SST), n-back task, task-fMRI (BOLD signal), CBCL for mental health
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The SST and n-back task were selected from the ABCD imaging battery to probe inhibitory control and working memory, respectively.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains: cognitive performance, brain activation, and mental health (CBCL).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear mixed models with site as random effect for behavioral outcomes; PALM permutation analysis for vertexwise fMRI comparisons; structural equation modeling for mediation
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race and ethnicity, IQ, puberty, combined parental income, scanner serial number, sibling status (neuroimaging only), site (random effect)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Linear mixed models were also used to compare VG and NVG on the 4 task-performance measures... These models included age, sex, race and ethnicity, IQ, puberty, and combined parental income as adjustment variables, and site as a random effect... False discovery rate (FDR) was assessed with the Benjamini and Hochberg procedure, and corrected P values and statistical maps were considered significant at P < .05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SMD; VG faster SSRT (287.3 vs 300.1 ms)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The adjusted means (SE) times for SSRT were 287.3 (9.8) vs 300.1 (9.6) milliseconds (SMD 0.04 milliseconds; P = .02)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SMD; VG faster go RT (514 vs 552 ms)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the adjusted means (SE) times for correct go RT were 514 (2.9) vs 552 (2.2) milliseconds (SMD, 0.5 milliseconds; P = .002)
the 0-back D' score was significantly higher in VGs relative to NVGs (adjusted means [SE], 2.33 [0.03] vs 2.18 [0.03]; P < .001)
2-back D' was significantly higher in VGs relative to NVGs (adjusted means [SE], 1.87 [0.03] vs 1.72 [0.02]; P < .002)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL t-score; VG higher attention problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: VGs had significantly higher attention problems, depression, and ADHD scores (FDR P < .05)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL t-score; VG higher depression scores
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: VGs had significantly higher attention problems, depression, and ADHD scores (FDR P < .05)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL t-score; VG higher ADHD scores; t-scores < 56 in both groups (not clinically significant)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: VGs had significantly higher attention problems, depression, and ADHD scores (FDR P < .05). The t scores from the CBCL were less than 56 in both groups and thus, none of the measures in either group was high enough to reach clinical significance

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: the video gamers had significantly higher attention problems, depression, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder scores compared with the those who did not play video games
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: the differences were very small and measured in fractions of milliseconds
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The abstract and title frame findings as 'enhanced cognitive performance' in video gamers. The paper does note effects are 'very small' in the abstract. SMDs range from 0.04 to 0.50. Mental health differences are subclinical (t-scores < 56).

3H_Recommendations.notes: The paper notes 'concerns about the association with mental health may warrant further study' but does not issue direct clinical or policy recommendations.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion presents video gaming as associated with very small cognitive performance improvements on inhibitory control and working memory tasks, with corresponding BOLD signal differences in relevant brain regions. The discussion notes that VGs also had higher depression, attention problems, and ADHD scores but that these were subclinical. The framing is relatively balanced, noting both potential cognitive benefits and mental health concerns, though the extreme-groups design (0 h/wk vs 21+ h/wk) limits generalizability.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Exact SMD values for CBCL outcomes not reported in text. The paper reports adjusted means and SEs but not always formal effect sizes for every comparison.
